1	---
2	name: blueprint-writer
3	description: Update one blueprint chapter to reflect strategy changes, fill missing definitions or theorems, or align prose with the current Lean structure. Plan-agent-dispatched; one writer per chapter. May spawn reference-retriever mid-session when drafting reveals a missing source.
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: true
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch one writer per chapter that the blueprint-reviewer's
10	    checklist flags as incomplete, lacking proof detail, or missing
11	    multi-route coverage.
12	  - Each directive must be precise: strategy context (the slice that
13	    matters for this chapter), required definitions/theorems (with
14	    enough mathematical detail to formalize), references, and
15	    explicit out-of-scope items. Writers do NOT speculate beyond
16	    what the directive lists.
17	  - **Authorize the retriever in the writer's --write-domain.** If
18	    the chapter might need fresh source material, dispatch the
19	    writer with TWO globs:
20	      --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter>.tex'
21	      --write-domain 'references/**'
22	    The writer itself only edits its assigned chapter (its prompt
23	    body enforces that), but the second glob authorizes a child
24	    reference-retriever if the writer discovers it needs one. Omit
25	    `references/**` only when you are confident the writer will not
26	    need new sources (e.g. purely cleanup edits).
27	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and one route's chapters
28	    are missing, dispatch a writer per route to bring all routes to
29	    parity before any prover work begins. Do not push provers onto a
30	    route whose blueprint coverage is incomplete.
31	  - After a significant writer round, re-dispatch blueprint-reviewer
32	    in the same iteration to confirm the updated blueprint is now
33	    sufficient — do not assume the writer fixed everything.
34	  - If a writer's report contains entries under "Strategy-modifying
35	    findings", STOP and update STRATEGY.md before any further Lean
36	    work this iter. The writer is telling you the prose surfaced a
37	    strategy-level issue.
38	  - If a writer's report includes child reference-retriever dispatches,
39	    skim the new `references/<slug>.md` files before using the
40	    writer's output — the writer relied on them, so should you.
41	---
42	
43	# Blueprint Writer
44	
45	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
46	
47	## Your Job
48	
49	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
50	
51	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
52	
53	## Directive Format
54	
55	```markdown
56	# Blueprint Writer Directive
57	
58	## Slug
59	<slug>
60	
61	## Target chapter
62	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
63	
64	## Strategy context
65	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
66	
67	## Required content
68	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
69	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
70	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
71	- ...
72	
73	## Out of scope
74	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
75	
76	## References
77	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
78	- ...
79	
80	## Expected outcome
81	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
82	```
83	
84	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
85	
86	## Chapter format
87	
88	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
89	
90	```latex
91	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
92	  \label{thm:some_label}
93	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
94	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
95	  Informal statement of the theorem, using standard mathematical notation.
96	\end{theorem}
97	
98	\begin{proof}
99	  \uses{thm:another_result}
100	  Step-by-step informal proof. Reference blueprint labels with \uses{...}
101	  so the dependency graph stays accurate.
102	\end{proof}
103	```
104	
105	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
106	
107	## Rules
108	
109	### What you CAN do
110	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
111	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
112	- Add `\uses{...}` cross-references.
113	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
114	
115	### What you MUST do
116	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
117	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
118	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
119	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
120	- **Document every change** in your report.
121	
122	### What you MUST NOT do
123	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` or `\mathlibok` markers.** Those are managed by the `sync_leanok` phase + the review agent — never by you.
124	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
125	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
126	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
127	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
128	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
129	
130	## Reading references
131	
132	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
133	
134	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
135	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
136	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
137	
138	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
139	
140	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
141	
142	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
143	
144	Conditions for dispatch:
145	
146	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
147	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
148	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
149	
150	Dispatch (Bash, foreground, in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
151	
152	```
153	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
154	  --name reference-retriever \
155	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
156	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
157	  --write-domain 'references/**'
158	```
159	
160	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
161	
162	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
163	
164	## Workflow
165	
166	1. Read your directive completely.
167	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references.
168	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
169	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow.
170	5. **If a needed source is missing from `references/`, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and wait for it to return before drafting the affected sections.
171	6. Make the edits.
172	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`).
173	8. Write your report.
174	
175	## Logging
176	
177	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-writer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
178	
179	```markdown
180	# Blueprint Writer Report
181	
182	## Slug
183	<slug>
184	
185	## Status
186	<COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE>
187	<If INCOMPLETE: which required items could not be written and why.>
188	
189	## Target chapter
190	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
191	
192	## Changes Made
193	- **Added definition** `\definition`/`\label{def:foo}`/`\lean{Foo.bar}` — <one line on what it captures>
194	- **Added theorem** `\theorem`/`\label{thm:foo}` — <one line on statement>
195	  - Proof sketch added: <Y/N + brief shape>
196	- **Revised** `<existing label>` — <one line on what changed>
197	- ...
198	
199	## Cross-references introduced
200	- `\uses{thm:bar}` added in proof of `\thm:foo` — verify `thm:bar` exists in <chapter or this same one>
201	- ...
202	
203	## Macros needed (if any)
204	- `\foo{...}` — used in <where>; needs definition in `macros/common.tex`. NOT added by me (out of write-domain).
205	
206	## Reference-retriever dispatches (if any)
207	- slug `<child-slug>`: requested `<source>`. Status: COMPLETE / NOT_FOUND / PARTIAL. Summary at `references/<slug>.md`.
208	- ...
209	
210	## Notes for Plan Agent
211	- <any inconsistency I noticed in sibling chapters that the directive didn't cover>
212	- <any structural concern: e.g. "the chapter is now 800 lines, consider splitting">
213	- <any reference I had to guess at because the directive's reference section was incomplete>
214	
215	## Strategy-modifying findings
216	<populate this section ONLY if writing the chapter surfaced a need to
217	change the project's strategy itself — not just the chapter content.
218	Examples:
219	- A definition you were asked to write turns out to require a property
220	  that the strategy assumes is automatic but is not.
221	- A theorem you sketched is provable as stated, but its consequence
222	  used elsewhere in STRATEGY.md does not follow from it.
223	- A reference cited as authoritative actually requires a different
224	  setup than the strategy assumes.
225	
226	When this section is non-empty, the plan agent must update STRATEGY.md
227	before any further Lean work this iteration. Do NOT silently adapt the
228	chapter to paper over a strategy-level issue — surface it here.>
229	```
230	
231	## Return Value
232	
233	Your final assistant message:
234	
235	- One line: `<slug>: COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE — <one-sentence outcome>`
236	- The path to your full report.
237	
238	Keep the inline return short. The plan agent reads the full report.
239

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are either the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (one of the descriptors in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	If instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**. Specifically:
8	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` (local to this project) override Archon's global prompts
9	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` (local to this project) override globally installed plugins
10	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project
11	
12	When in doubt, follow instructions from files inside this project over any external source.
13	
14	## Skills
15	- archon-lean4: installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source) — provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, and other Lean4 commands
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List them with `ls .claude/tools/` and run `<path> --help` to learn each tool's purpose and usage. Invoke them via the Bash tool. Project MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for informal mathematical reasoning. Useful when you need a second opinion or a paper-style proof sketch the Lean side can then formalize.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (see "Subagents" below). One wrapper handles every subagent.
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`. Each starts with YAML frontmatter (`name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`) followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude session reads.
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents yourself.** The plan and review prompts have an auto-generated **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation that lists every enabled descriptor with its description, write-domain hint, and any `[MANDATORY]` flags. When you decide to invoke a specific subagent, read `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` for its full prompt and directive shape.
31	
32	**Invoke any subagent with the generic wrapper** (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, and writes the subagent's report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. It shells out to `archon subagent <name>`, so executions stream through the Archon JSONL log like a phase agent. The dispatch semaphore bounds total concurrent subagent processes by `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` automatically so deeper-nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Always dispatch synchronously** (not via background `Bash` with `run_in_background: true`). The wrapper returns when the subagent has finished and the report is on disk; only then write your session summary. Background dispatch leaves the parent's session permanently summarized as "running in background", which is wrong as soon as the subagent completes.
45	
46	**Mandatory subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` MUST be dispatched at least once during the plan phase (similarly for `[review]`). The catalog tags them `[MANDATORY]`. A post-phase audit warns when a mandatory dispatch is missing.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All state files are in `.archon/`:
51	
52	| File | Plan Agent | Prover Agent | Subagents | Review Agent | User |
53	|------|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|------|
54	| `.archon/PROGRESS.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
55	| `.archon/STRATEGY.md` | **read + write** | do not read | do not read | do not read | read |
56	| `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` | read (then clear) | do not read | do not read | do not read | write |
57	| `.archon/task_pending.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
58	| `.archon/task_done.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
59	| `.archon/task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect results) | write (own file only) | write (`<name>-<slug>.md`) | read only | read |
60	| `.archon/proof-journal/` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
61	| `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log moved to iter/iter-NNN/review.md) | read |
62	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | do not access | do not access | read (last K iters as context) | read |
63	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (last K iters as context) | do not access | do not access | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write (per-attempt detail) | do not access | do not access | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | **read** | **read** | **read** (write-capable subagents may rename file path only) | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | do not edit | write (own file only, frozen protected signatures) | per descriptor: write-capable subagents (e.g. refactor) may edit per their declared write-domain (protected decls may be moved but not renamed/re-signed); read-only subagents do not edit | do not edit | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints, structure) | do not edit | do not edit | **write** (markers only: `\leanok`, `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections) | read |
68	| `Challenges/<Name>.lean` | do not edit | fill sorries (when assigned) | write-capable when the descriptor's write_domain covers it | do not edit | read |
69	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | do not access | write-capable when the descriptor's write_domain covers it | do not access | read |
70	
71	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow, run by hand by the mathematician.** The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. The plan agent invokes the refactor subagent (when a `refactor` descriptor is installed) via the generic `archon-subagent.py --name refactor` wrapper with a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/`. This keeps each loop-driven refactor independently logged and avoids any chance of stale-directive reuse. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`, etc.) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise and prune them out as you rewrite.
72	
73	## Protected Declarations
74	
75	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Every agent must consult it before editing a `.lean` file. Rules:
76	
77	- **Plan / prover / review agents**: read-only on protected signatures. You may fill proof bodies, but not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
78	- **Refactor agent**: may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (keeping name + signature verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. Refactor agents may never rename, re-type, delete, or re-sign a protected declaration.
79	- The `archon-protected.yaml` file can only be edited by the user, no declaration can be added or removed by any agent. The only allowed modification is updating the file path of an existing protected declaration when its location was changed.
80	
81	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
82	
83	The blueprint uses two active markers.
84	
85	- `\leanok` — inside a statement block when the declaration is formalized with at least a `sorry`; inside a proof block when the proof is fully closed with no `sorry`.
86	- `\mathlibok` — inside a statement block when the declaration already exists in Mathlib and the Archon side is a re-export/alias; no Archon proof obligation remains.
87	- No marker — the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
88	
89	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between the prover and review phases each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves — let the script do it.
90	
91	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations remain the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
92	
93	## User Interaction
94	
95	Users provide hints in two places:
96	
97	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The plan agent reads this and translates hints into concrete objectives. Provers never read this file.
98	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
99	
100	## Agent Roles
101	
102	### Plan Agent
103	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for your full instructions
104	- Read `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate hints, then clear them after acting
105	- Read `.archon/task_results/` — collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md`
106	- Optionally invoke **subagents** (discoverable via `ls .archon/subagents/`) by running `.claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name <name> ...` via Bash, **before** writing prover objectives. See `.archon/prompts/plan.md` § "Subagent delegation" for the canonical ordering and full invocation pattern.
107	- Archive any subagent report you receive to `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` so the dashboard can render it.
108	- Write `.archon/PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round
109	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (except for marker updates) and `\lean{...}` hints for the provers
110	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself
111	
112	### Prover Agent
113	- Read `.archon/PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it)
114	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/`:
115	  - autoformalize → `.archon/prompts/prover-autoformalize.md`
116	  - prover → `.archon/prompts/prover-prover.md`
117	  - polish → `.archon/prompts/prover-polish.md`
118	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md`
119	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — **never edit another agent's file**
120	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your `.lean` file for file-specific hints
121	- **Do NOT edit blueprint chapters.** Marker updates are the review agent's responsibility. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker.
122	
123	### Subagents
124	
125	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime — there is no fixed roster baked into this file. Each lives at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter + prompt body). To see what's available **this iteration**, run `ls .archon/subagents/` and read each descriptor's frontmatter for purpose, write-domain hint, and directive shape.
126	
127	Subagents are **not** part of the autonomous loop's standing phases. Phase agents (plan, review) invoke them on demand via the generic wrapper:
128	
129	```
130	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name <name> --slug <slug> --directive-file <path> --write-domain '<glob>'
131	```
132	
133	Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, or other phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. The dispatching agent reads the report when the wrapper returns and may then update `STRATEGY.md` / `PROGRESS.md` based on the findings.
134	
135	Some descriptors set `can_spawn: true`, meaning that subagent may itself dispatch children; the per-iter dispatch semaphore caps total concurrent processes by `loop.max_parallel` so deep trees stay bounded.
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for your full instructions
139	- Read `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data
140	- Write session journal to `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (summary.md, milestones.jsonl, recommendations.md)
141	- Update `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` with overall progress
142	- Maintain the **semantic** blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE: ...` annotations, stale `\notready` removal — in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch `\leanok`; it's handled by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase that ran before you.

37	## Decomposition: genus-stratified body of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`
38	
39	The protected `nonempty_jacobianWitness` has signature
40	
41	```
42	theorem nonempty_jacobianWitness (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
43	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
44	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] :
45	    Nonempty (JacobianWitness C)
46	```
47	
48	Although signatures are frozen, bodies are not. The plan is to
49	restructure the proof body as a genus case-split:
50	
51	```
52	theorem nonempty_jacobianWitness ... := by
53	  by_cases h : AlgebraicGeometry.genus (k := k) C.left = 0
54	  · exact ⟨genusZeroWitness C h⟩      -- closed by milestone M2
55	  · exact ⟨positiveGenusWitness C (Nat.pos_of_ne_zero h)⟩  -- closed by M3
56	```
57	
58	`genus C : ℕ` is decidable-equality, so the `by_cases` is well-formed.
59	This converts M2 from "preparatory infrastructure" into an explicit
60	half-of-a-sorry-closure: the genus-0 sub-theorem `genusZeroWitness`
61	becomes a concrete Lean target. M3 is then framed as "close

97	**Estimated cost.** 3–6 iter / 130–300 LOC (revised downward from

101	**Statement.** Let `f : X ⟶ S` be a morphism of schemes, `U : S.Opens`

105	cotangent presheaf at `V` is canonically `B`-linearly isomorphic to
106	the appLE-algebra Kähler module:

108	```
109	(relativeDifferentialsPresheaf f).presheaf.obj (.op V)  ≃ₗ[B]  Ω[B ⁄ A]
110	```

142	### M2 — Genus-0 witness sub-theorem `genusZeroWitness`
143	
144	**Estimated cost.** 5–10 iter / 250–600 LOC (LOC range updated upward
145	from the iter-121 first draft per strategy-critic's effort-honesty
146	finding; the base-change-and-descent infrastructure is non-trivial).
147	
148	**Statement.** Let `C` be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
149	curve over a field `k` with `genus C = 0`. Then there exists a
150	`JacobianWitness` for `C` whose underlying scheme is `Spec k`.
151	
152	**Critical correction from iter-121 first draft.** The first-draft
153	strategy named M2.c as `C ≅ ℙ¹_k`, which is **mathematically false**
154	for the protected signature: Brauer–Severi conics over `ℚ` (smooth
155	proper geometrically irreducible curves of genus 0 without
156	`ℚ`-rational points) are *not* isomorphic to `ℙ¹_ℚ`. The corrected M2
157	handles the non-`k`-rational-point case by base change to the
158	algebraic closure `k̄`:
159	
160	- Over `k̄`, every smooth proper geom-irred curve of genus 0 has a
161	  `k̄`-rational point (geom-irred + nonempty source ⇒ generic point
162	  pulls back to a closed `k̄`-rational point on `C_{k̄}` via
163	  `geomIrred.exists_kalg_pt` or equivalent — the standard
164	  $k$-rational-point existence on the geometric fibre).
165	- `C_{k̄} ≅ ℙ¹_{k̄}` via the genus-0-with-rational-point step (this
166	  is now over `k̄`, where the identification holds; Mathlib needs
167	  Riemann–Roch over algebraically closed fields, still upstream
168	  work).
169	- Rigidity for `ℙ¹_{k̄} → A_{k̄}` (project's existing
170	  `GrpObj.eq_of_eqOnOpen` applied over `k̄`).
171	- Galois descent of constancy back to `k`.
172	
173	For the genus-0 universal property of `J = Spec k`:
174	
175	- If `C(k) ≠ ∅`: pick a marked point `P ∈ C(k)`. The unique morphism
176	  `ι_P : C → Spec k` is `terminal`; the factorisation claim for
177	  `f : C → A` with `f(P) = e_A` reduces to "f is constant at e_A,"
178	  which follows from rigidity (after base change to `k̄` and descent).
179	- If `C(k) = ∅` (Brauer–Severi case): `IsAlbanese C P J` is vacuously
180	  satisfied for every `P` (there are no marked points to
181	  universally-quantify over); the `JacobianWitness` field
182	  `isAlbaneseFor : ∀ P ∈ C(k), IsAlbanese C P J` is therefore vacuous.
183	
184	**Decomposition.**
185	
186	| Step | Math content | Mathlib leverage | Est. |
187	|---|---|---|---|
188	| M2.a | Rigidity for `ℙ¹_{k̄} → A_{k̄}`: any morphism from `ℙ¹_{k̄}` to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme `A_{k̄}` over `k̄` that hits the identity at a $k̄$-rational point is constant | The project's existing `GrpObj.eq_of_eqOnOpen` over `k̄` (Rigidity.lean) | 2–3 iter / 100 LOC |
189	| M2.b | Genus-0 witness for `Spec k`: define `genusZeroWitness C h` where `h : genus C = 0`, returning a `JacobianWitness C` with underlying scheme `Spec k`, trivial group structure, smoothness of relative dimension 0 (matching `genus C = 0`), and `isAlbaneseFor` either via vacuity (no $k$-rational points) or via the base-change-and-descent argument (one $k$-rational point exists) | New project material | 2 iter / 100 LOC |
190	| M2.c | Base-change-and-descent infrastructure: `(C ⊗_k k̄).hom : C_{k̄} → Spec k̄`; group-scheme structure of `A_{k̄}`; rigidity-and-constancy descent | Mathlib: `Scheme.Pullback`, `Spec.preserves` on group-scheme structures. Project must verify whether Mathlib has scheme-level Galois-descent of morphism equality | 3–6 iter / 150–300 LOC |
191	| M2.d | Genus-0 identification `C_{k̄} ≅ ℙ¹_{k̄}` over `k̄`: requires `H⁰(C_{k̄}, O_{C_{k̄}}(P)) = 2` for some $k̄$-rational point $P$ (Riemann-Roch consequence over $k̄$). This is the genus-0 identification *over the algebraic closure*; the project's existing genus-via-Ext definition needs the Riemann–Roch-over-algebraically-closed-field step. Mathlib `b80f227` has no usable Riemann–Roch even over algebraically closed fields | Mathlib gap; partial coverage in `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.lean` / `Cohomology_MayerVietorisCore.lean` for the cohomology side; the divisor-side input is the genuine gap | 5–10 iter / 250–500 LOC |
192	
193	The base-change-and-descent (M2.c) is the realistic gating cost. The
194	strategy-critic correctly identified that the iter-121 first draft
195	under-counted by ignoring the non-$k$-rational-point case; this
196	revised M2 accounts for it.
197	
198	**Sub-step dependency**: M2.b depends on M2.a (rigidity). M2.c is a
199	prerequisite for M2.a's transport from `k̄` to `k`. M2.d gates the
200	case `C(k) ≠ ∅` (the `C_{k̄} ≅ ℙ¹_{k̄}` identification is what makes
201	M2.a applicable). Concretely: M2.a can be attempted first
202	(stand-alone over `k̄`); M2.c and M2.d are sequential prerequisites
203	for the `Spec k`-side conclusion.

205	### M3 — Positive-genus witness sub-theorem `positiveGenusWitness`
206	
207	**Estimated cost.** Many tens of iters / thousands of LOC; multi-month
208	build-out.
209	
210	**Statement.** Let `C` be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
211	curve over `k` with `genus C ≥ 1`. Then there exists a
212	`JacobianWitness` for `C` whose underlying scheme is the Albanese
213	variety of `C` (i.e. the connected component of the identity of
214	`Pic_{C/k}`, or equivalently the Stein factorisation of the
215	Abel–Jacobi morphism on `Sym^g(C)`).
216	
217	**Two equally hard routes.** The project will pick one route after
218	M1 closes and the project audits Mathlib's then-current snapshot
219	against each route's gating dependencies.
220	
221	#### Route A — Picard scheme via FGA
222	
223	Hilbert schemes, Quot schemes, representability of the Picard
224	functor for smooth proper geometrically connected curves,
225	identity-component construction.
226	
227	**Top-3 gating Mathlib pieces** (each is itself a multi-thousand-LOC
228	contribution candidate):
229	
230	1. **Hilbert scheme representability for projective schemes**:
231	   `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Hilbert.Representability` (doesn't
232	   exist). Decomposes into (a) the Hilbert functor as a functor
233	   $(\Sch/k)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \Sets$, (b) the representability theorem
234	   via Grothendieck's flattening stratification, (c) the
235	   smoothness/properness of the Hilbert scheme on smooth projective
236	   bases.
237	2. **Quot scheme representability**: representability of the Quot
238	   functor of coherent quotients on a proper scheme. Generalises
239	   Hilbert; depends on coherent-sheaf-of-finite-type infrastructure
240	   that is partially in Mathlib.
241	3. **Identity-component construction `G^0 ⊆ G`**: for a $k$-group
242	   scheme `G` locally of finite type, the connected component of the
243	   identity as a closed (and open) subgroup scheme. Requires
244	   `IsConnectedSpace` for the identity component, plus the topological
245	   connectedness of identity components of group objects in `Scheme/k`.
246	
247	#### Route B — Symmetric powers + Stein factorisation
248	
249	Scheme-level symmetric powers, finite-group-scheme quotients,
250	Brill–Noether–Riemann–Roch, Stein factorisation.
251	
252	**Top-3 gating Mathlib pieces**:
253	
254	1. **Symmetric powers of schemes `Sym^n X` with smoothness**:
255	   `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.SymmetricPower` (doesn't exist).
256	   Requires the finite-group-quotient construction `X^n / S_n` with
257	   smoothness when `X` is smooth (Fogarty's symmetric-product
258	   computation).
259	2. **Stein factorisation theorem**: for a proper morphism
260	   `f : X → Y` of locally Noetherian schemes, $f_* \mathcal O_X$ is a
261	   coherent $\mathcal O_Y$-algebra and $f$ factors as
262	   $X \to \mathrm{Spec}_Y(f_*\mathcal O_X) \to Y$. Requires coherent
263	   $\mathcal O$-module cohomology of proper morphisms (partially in
264	   Mathlib).
265	3. **Brill–Noether and Riemann–Roch**: as for M2.d, the curve-side
266	   Riemann–Roch input is the largest Mathlib gap; absent from
267	   Mathlib in usable form for the project's genus-via-Ext
268	   definition.
269	
270	#### Route-pick decision criterion
271	
272	**Trigger.** Route is picked at the start of the first iter after M1
273	closes and the project's Mathlib snapshot is audited against the
274	above gating dependencies.
275	
276	**Criterion.** Run a Mathlib coverage audit of each route's top-3
277	gating pieces. For each missing piece, estimate the project-internal
278	LOC required to build it (the typical multi-K LOC contribution). Pick
279	the route with the smaller cumulative LOC estimate. **Tiebreaker**:
280	if estimates are within 20% of each other, prefer the route whose
281	top-3 gating pieces have the most cross-utility outside the Jacobian
282	arc (the Hilbert/Quot/representability infrastructure of Route A has
283	broader downstream value; the symmetric-power / Stein machinery of
284	Route B is more curve-specific). **Hard fallback**: if both routes
285	exceed 5000 LOC of estimated upstream-Mathlib work, escalate to the
286	user for an external-PR routing decision (likely: post an upstream
287	PR for at least the smallest gating piece and continue on the
288	remaining routes-and-arms while waiting).
289	
290	#### Per-iter progress signal for M3
291	
292	During M3's multi-iter run, the progress signal is:
293	
294	- **Iter $N$ "on track" criteria**:
295	  1. At least one named gating piece has its scaffolding declaration
296	     (with `sorry` body) introduced or its body partially closed.
297	  2. The cumulative LOC against the route's bill-of-materials
298	     decreases or stays flat (an iter that ADDS LOC to the BOM is
299	     CHURNING; the route is on a deeper gap than estimated).
300	  3. The cumulative `\leanok`-tagged declarations across the BOM
301	     increases by at least one per 2 iters (otherwise CHURNING).
302	- **Iter "off track" signal**:
303	  1. Two iters in a row introduce a new helper declaration whose
304	     blueprint chapter doesn't yet exist (blueprint expansion is
305	     overdue).
306	  2. The prover lane on the BOM's smallest piece returns INCOMPLETE
307	     twice consecutively (the piece is too tightly coupled to
308	     Mathlib infrastructure; pivot strategy or escalate to user).

342	## Soundness rules
343	
344	- **No new axioms.** Every closed declaration must `lean_verify` to
345	  kernel-only axioms (`propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound`).
346	- **No "deferred" framing.** Mathlib gaps are decomposed into the
347	  M1/M2/M3 roadmap with concrete sub-step estimates; the planner does
348	  not write "out-of-autonomous-loop scope" sections anymore. If a
349	  sub-step is genuinely outside the loop's reach (e.g. requires a
350	  multi-month upstream contribution), it is recorded in the roadmap
351	  with an explicit "blocked on upstream X" note and a fallback
352	  iter-by-iter approach that progresses the surrounding sub-steps.
353	- **No phantom $k$-rational-point hypotheses.** The protected
354	  `nonempty_jacobianWitness` quantifies over arbitrary `C` without
355	  $k$-rational points. Sub-strategies that depend on $C(k) \neq \emptyset$
356	  (notably `C ≅ ℙ¹_k`) must explicitly handle the no-rational-point
357	  case via base change to $\bar k$ + Galois descent, or document why
358	  vacuity (`isAlbaneseFor` is vacuously true when $C(k) = \emptyset$)
359	  suffices.
360	- **Converse of `smooth_locally_free_omega` is mathematically false.**

1	\chapter{Rigidity for morphisms of group schemes (Mumford \S4)}
2	\label{chap:Rigidity}
3	
4	This chapter records a self-contained helper: \emph{rigidity for morphisms of abelian varieties}. It is the only sorry-blocking lemma in the project that is provable from current Mathlib alone, and it unlocks the uniqueness half of Theorem~\ref{thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp} (the Albanese property).
5	
6	The lemma is independent of the construction of $\Jac(C)$ --- the source and target are abstract proper smooth geometrically irreducible group schemes over the base field $k$. It is therefore the highest-leverage standalone target for a prover round.
7	
8	\section{Statement}
9	
10	\begin{theorem}\leanok
11	[Rigidity for morphisms of group schemes]
12	  \label{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.eq_of_eqOnOpen}
14	  Let $k$ be a field. Let $X, Y$ be smooth proper geometrically irreducible group schemes over $k$. Let $g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y$ be two morphisms of $k$-schemes, and let $U \subseteq X$ be a non-empty open subset. If
15	  \[
16	    g_1\big|_U \;=\; g_2\big|_U
17	  \]
18	  on the underlying topological points, then $g_1 = g_2$ as morphisms of $k$-schemes.
19	\end{theorem}
20	
21	\begin{remark}
22	  The classical Mumford rigidity theorem usually states this for morphisms vanishing on an open subset (the difference morphism $g_1 - g_2$ vanishes on $U$, hence is identically zero). Our formulation above is the symmetric ``agree on $U$ $\Rightarrow$ agree everywhere'' version; it is interderivable with the standard statement using the group law on $Y$.
23	\end{remark}
24	
25	\section{Proof sketch}
26	
27	\begin{proof}
28	  \leanok
29	  Reduce to the standard form: let $\delta \colon X \to Y$ be the difference morphism $\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}$, which makes sense because $Y$ carries a group-object structure. The hypothesis says $\delta$ takes the constant value $\eta[Y]$ (the identity of $Y$) on $U$.
30	
31	  Consider the equaliser
32	  \[
33	    E \;=\; \mathrm{eq}\bigl(\delta,\; (\mathtt{toUnit}\ X) \circ \eta[Y]\bigr) \;\hookrightarrow\; X,
34	  \]
35	  i.e.\ the closed subscheme of $X$ on which $\delta$ agrees with the constant identity morphism. Two facts:
36	  \begin{enumerate}
37	    \item \emph{$E$ is closed in $X$.} The target $Y$ is separated over $\Spec k$ (proper $\Rightarrow$ separated), so the equaliser of any two morphisms into $Y$ is closed in the source.
38	    \item \emph{$E$ contains the open $U$.} By hypothesis on the underlying points; promote to a scheme-theoretic statement using the reduced-induced subscheme structure on $U$ and faithfulness of the underlying-set functor on reduced schemes.
39	  \end{enumerate}
40	  Since $X$ is geometrically irreducible, in particular irreducible, the closed subscheme $E$ contains a non-empty open $U$, hence equals all of $X$ as a topological space. Combined with the fact that both $X$ and $Y$ are reduced (smooth $\Rightarrow$ regular $\Rightarrow$ reduced), and the morphisms $\delta$ and $\mathtt{toUnit}\ X \circ \eta[Y]$ are determined by their values on the underlying topological space (because $X$ is reduced and $Y$ is separated), we conclude $\delta = \mathtt{toUnit}\ X \circ \eta[Y]$, i.e.\ $g_1 = g_2$.
41	
42	  \medskip
43	  \noindent\textbf{Mathlib ingredients.}
44	  \begin{itemize}
45	    \item Proper morphisms are separated.
46	    \item The equaliser-is-closed property for morphisms into a separated $k$-scheme.
47	    \item The underlying topological space of a geometrically irreducible scheme is irreducible.
48	    \item Reducedness for $X$ and $Y$, plus the lemma identifying scheme morphisms by their values on points (for reduced source and separated target).
49	    \item Smoothness implies regularity, hence reducedness.
50	    \item The group-object structure on $Y$ to form the difference morphism $\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}$, and to access $\eta[Y]$.
51	    \item Mathlib's abelian-variety commutativity result is \emph{not} used in the proof, but provides the wider context (commutativity of the group law).
52	  \end{itemize}
53	\end{proof}
54	
55	\section{Use in the project}
56	
57	The rigidity theorem is used in the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp} (the Albanese property of the Jacobian). Specifically: given two morphisms $g_1, g_2 \colon \Jac(C) \to A$ with $g_1 \circ \alpha_P = g_2 \circ \alpha_P$, the rigidity theorem applied to $\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}$ on the image of $\alpha_P$ (a non-empty open after passing to the symmetric power $C^{(g)} \twoheadrightarrow \Jac(C)$) forces $g_1 = g_2$.
58	
59	Note: the existence half of \ref{thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp} requires Phase~B/C infrastructure (Picard scheme, dual abelian variety). Rigidity alone yields \emph{uniqueness} only.
60	
61	\section{Mathlib gap}
62	
63	No rigidity lemma exists in current Mathlib. The closest pre-existing declaration is commutativity of proper geometrically integral group schemes (Stacks 0BFD). Our rigidity statement is independent of and in fact compatible with this commutativity result.
64	
65	The proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} uses only Mathlib infrastructure that is already present (separatedness from properness, equaliser closedness, irreducibility of source, reducedness from smoothness). It is therefore provable in this iteration.
66

input.old_string: \item[\textbf{(C.2)}] \emph{Rigidity for morphisms from $\mathbb P^1_k$ to an abelian variety.} Let $A$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over $k$ and let $f \colon \mathbb P^1_k \to A$ satisfy $f(P) = \eta_A$ for some $P \in \mathbb P^1_k(k)$. Then $f$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$. Equivalently, $\Hom(\mathbb P^1_k, A) = A(k)$, with the bijection sending a constant morphism to its value. The classical proof uses the rigidity lemma: any morphism from a proper geometrically connected variety with a $k$-rational point to a separated $k$-scheme that contracts a fibre to a point is constant; for $\mathbb P^1_k \to A$ this fibre is all of $\mathbb P^1_k$ over the identity of $A$.
input.new_string:
\item[\textbf{(C.2)}] \emph{Rigidity for morphisms from $\mathbb P^1$ to an abelian variety.} We state the rigidity result over the algebraic closure $\bar k$, since the protected signature of Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} does not assume $C(k) \neq \emptyset$ and the genus-$0$ identification $C \cong \mathbb P^1$ is only available after passing to $\bar k$ (Brauer--Severi conics over $\mathbb Q$ are the standard counterexamples to a $k$-side identification without a rational point). The conclusion over $k$ --- needed only in the sub-case $C(k) \neq \emptyset$, where Sub-step~C.1 gives $C \cong \mathbb P^1_k$ already over $k$ --- then follows by Galois descent (Sub-step~C.2.f).

    \begin{itemize}
      \item[\textbf{(C.2.a)}] \emph{Statement (over $\bar k$).} Let $A$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over $\bar k$, with identity element $\eta_A \in A(\bar k)$. Let
      \[
        f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \longrightarrow A
      \]
      be a morphism of $\bar k$-schemes such that $f(p) = \eta_A$ for some closed point $p \in \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}(\bar k)$. Then $f$ factors as the composite $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to \Spec \bar k \xrightarrow{\,\eta_A\,} A$; equivalently, $f$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$.

      \item[\textbf{(C.2.b)}] \emph{Reduction to the project's rigidity lemma.} Let $c \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ denote the constant morphism at $\eta_A$ --- the unique $\bar k$-morphism factoring through the closed-point section $\eta_A \colon \Spec \bar k \hookrightarrow A$. The conclusion $f = c$ would follow from Theorem~\ref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} applied to $g_1 := f$, $g_2 := c$, $X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$, $Y := A$, once we produce a non-empty open $U \subseteq \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ on which $f|_U = c|_U$ as scheme morphisms. The cited theorem is stated for $X$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme, whereas $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ is not a group scheme; however, the group-object structure on $X$ is used in the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} \emph{only} to form a difference morphism $\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}$, and the underlying equaliser-closed argument (separated target $\Rightarrow$ equaliser closed; irreducible source plus reduced source plus separated target $\Rightarrow$ scheme equality from set-level equality on an open) goes through verbatim with $X = \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. The reduction therefore amounts to: produce a non-empty open $U \subseteq \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ on which $f$ and $c$ agree as scheme morphisms; we will in fact take $U = \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.

      \item[\textbf{(C.2.c)}] \emph{Image dimension argument.} The morphism $f$ goes from a proper geometrically integral $\bar k$-scheme of dimension $1$ to a smooth proper $\bar k$-scheme of dimension $g := \dim_{\bar k} A$. The set-theoretic image $f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) \subseteq A$ is an irreducible closed subset of $A$ (the continuous image of an irreducible space under a proper map is irreducible and closed) of dimension at most $1$. If $g = 0$ then $A = \Spec \bar k$ and the conclusion is vacuous: the unique $\bar k$-morphism to $\Spec \bar k$ is the constant morphism. Henceforth assume $g \geq 1$, in which case the image is either a single point (the desired conclusion) or a one-dimensional irreducible proper closed subscheme of $A$ --- an irreducible rational curve in $A$.

      \item[\textbf{(C.2.d)}] \emph{Key classical input: proper rational curves on an abelian variety are constant.} The remaining case --- image of dimension exactly $1$ --- is ruled out by the following classical fact (Mumford, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Chapter~II~\S4, Proposition):

      \begin{quote}
        \emph{Let $A$ be an abelian variety of positive dimension over an algebraically closed field $\bar k$. Then every morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant.}
      \end{quote}

      Two standard proofs are available; both unwind a few layers of upstream Mathlib infrastructure but are routine once the input lemmas are present.
      \begin{itemize}
        \item \emph{Via the dual abelian variety.} Pullback of line bundles along $f$ induces a homomorphism of group schemes $f^{\vee} \colon A^{\vee} \to \Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})$, where $A^{\vee}$ is the dual abelian variety (the moduli space of degree-zero line bundles on $A$). The Picard group of $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ has trivial degree-zero component, $\Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) = 0$, because every line bundle on $\mathbb P^1$ of degree $0$ is isomorphic to $\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}$ (Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6 and II.7). Hence $f^{\vee} = 0$. By autoduality of abelian varieties ($A^{\vee\vee} = A$ canonically, double duality of the Poincar\'e line bundle), the zero of $f^{\vee}$ implies that $f$ itself is constant.

        \item \emph{Via the cotangent bundle.} The cotangent sheaf $\Omega_{A/\bar k}$ of an abelian variety is a trivial $\mathcal O_A$-module: the tangent bundle of a group scheme over a field is generated globally by translates of the Lie algebra at the identity, hence is trivial, hence so is its dual. Pulling back along $f$ yields a morphism of locally free sheaves $f^{*} \Omega_{A/\bar k} \to \Omega_{\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}/\bar k}$, whose global sections form a $\bar k$-linear map
        \[
          H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k}\bigr) \longrightarrow H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, \Omega_{\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}/\bar k}\bigr) = H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, \mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(-2)\bigr) = 0.
        \]
        Triviality of $\Omega_{A/\bar k}$ means the left-hand side is a non-zero $\bar k$-vector space (of dimension $g$); the only way the map can land in zero is for it to be the zero map at every fibre, i.e.\ for the differential $df$ to vanish identically on $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. Smoothness of $A$ over $\bar k$ together with the vanishing of $df$ forces $f$ to be a constant morphism on the geometric points and hence (by reduced-source / separated-target, see C.2.e) as a scheme morphism.
      \end{itemize}
      Either proof yields: $f$ is constant. The pointed condition $f(p) = \eta_A$ then identifies the constant value, so $f = c$ on underlying topological points.

      \item[\textbf{(C.2.e)}] \emph{Promoting set-level equality to scheme equality.} The set-level equality $f = c$ on underlying topological points (established in C.2.d) promotes to scheme-morphism equality on any non-empty open $U \subseteq \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via the standard reduced-source / separated-target argument: $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ is reduced (smooth implies regular, regular implies reduced) and $A$ is separated over $\bar k$ (proper implies separated), so a $\bar k$-morphism $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is determined by its values on the underlying topological space. In particular, $f|_U = c|_U$ as scheme morphisms for the open $U := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ itself, and the reduction of C.2.b gives $f = c$ as scheme morphisms.

      \item[\textbf{(C.2.f)}] \emph{Galois descent to $k$.} For the sub-case $C(k) \neq \emptyset$, fix a marked point $P \in C(k)$; Sub-step~C.1 then gives a $k$-scheme isomorphism $C \cong \mathbb P^1_k$, and we have to conclude that every $k$-morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_k \to A$ with $f(P) = \eta_A$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$. The base change $f_{\bar k} \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}$ satisfies the hypotheses of C.2.a (with $p := P_{\bar k}$), hence is constant by the $\bar k$-side conclusion. Constancy descends from $\bar k$ to $k$: two morphisms of $k$-schemes $f, c \colon \mathbb P^1_k \to A$ are equal if and only if their base changes $f_{\bar k}, c_{\bar k}$ to $\bar k$ are equal. This descent uses that $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ is faithfully flat (and in fact pro-Galois), and that morphism equality is local in the fpqc topology --- the trivial descent datum on a single morphism descends along any faithfully flat extension. The result is: every $k$-morphism $\mathbb P^1_k \to A$ with $f(P) = \eta_A$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$.

      \item[\textbf{(C.2.g)}] \emph{Mathlib gap statement.} The keystone fact of Sub-step~C.2.d --- that every morphism from $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ to a positive-dimensional abelian variety over an algebraically closed field is constant --- is \emph{not} present in current Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) in any form usable here. The project will introduce it as a Lean-side declaration along the lines of
      \[
        \mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.AbelianVariety.constant\_of\_P1\_map}
      \]
      (final name and namespace to be fixed when the declaration is introduced into the Lean tree; the project intends this lemma as an upstream-Mathlib contribution candidate). When $g = \dim A = 0$ the statement is vacuous and the declaration's conclusion is automatic. The reduction step C.2.b reuses the project's already-formalised Theorem~\ref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} (with the noted ``group-object-on-source'' hypothesis handled either by a thin variant of the lemma or by inlining its proof template); the promotion step C.2.e uses standard reduced-source / separated-target lemmas, partially in Mathlib; the descent step C.2.f requires scheme-level Galois descent of morphism equality, also a Mathlib gap and itself a contribution candidate.
    \end{itemize}

342	        \item \emph{Via the cotangent bundle.} The cotangent sheaf $\Omega_{A/\bar k}$ of an abelian variety is a trivial $\mathcal O_A$-module: the tangent bundle of a group scheme over a field is generated globally by translates of the Lie algebra at the identity, hence is trivial, hence so is its dual. Pulling back along $f$ yields a morphism of locally free sheaves $f^{*} \Omega_{A/\bar k} \to \Omega_{\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}/\bar k}$, whose global sections form a $\bar k$-linear map
343	        \[
344	          H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k}\bigr) \longrightarrow H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, \Omega_{\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}/\bar k}\bigr) = H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, \mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(-2)\bigr) = 0.
345	        \]
346	        Triviality of $\Omega_{A/\bar k}$ means the left-hand side is a non-zero $\bar k$-vector space (of dimension $g$); the only way the map can land in zero is for it to be the zero map at every fibre, i.e.\ for the differential $df$ to vanish identically on $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. Smoothness of $A$ over $\bar k$ together with the vanishing of $df$ forces $f$ to be a constant morphism on the geometric points and hence (by reduced-source / separated-target, see C.2.e) as a scheme morphism.
347	      \end{itemize}
348	      Either proof yields: $f$ is constant. The pointed condition $f(p) = \eta_A$ then identifies the constant value, so $f = c$ on underlying topological points.
349	
350	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.e)}] \emph{Promoting set-level equality to scheme equality.} The set-level equality $f = c$ on underlying topological points (established in C.2.d) promotes to scheme-morphism equality on any non-empty open $U \subseteq \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via the standard reduced-source / separated-target argument: $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ is reduced (smooth implies regular, regular implies reduced) and $A$ is separated over $\bar k$ (proper implies separated), so a $\bar k$-morphism $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is determined by its values on the underlying topological space. In particular, $f|_U = c|_U$ as scheme morphisms for the open $U := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ itself, and the reduction of C.2.b gives $f = c$ as scheme morphisms.
351	
352	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.f)}] \emph{Galois descent to $k$.} For the sub-case $C(k) \neq \emptyset$, fix a marked point $P \in C(k)$; Sub-step~C.1 then gives a $k$-scheme isomorphism $C \cong \mathbb P^1_k$, and we have to conclude that every $k$-morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_k \to A$ with $f(P) = \eta_A$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$. The base change $f_{\bar k} \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}$ satisfies the hypotheses of C.2.a (with $p := P_{\bar k}$), hence is constant by the $\bar k$-side conclusion. Constancy descends from $\bar k$ to $k$: two morphisms of $k$-schemes $f, c \colon \mathbb P^1_k \to A$ are equal if and only if their base changes $f_{\bar k}, c_{\bar k}$ to $\bar k$ are equal. This descent uses that $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ is faithfully flat (and in fact pro-Galois), and that morphism equality is local in the fpqc topology --- the trivial descent datum on a single morphism descends along any faithfully flat extension. The result is: every $k$-morphism $\mathbb P^1_k \to A$ with $f(P) = \eta_A$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$.
353	
354	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.g)}] \emph{Mathlib gap statement.} The keystone fact of Sub-step~C.2.d --- that every morphism from $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ to a positive-dimensional abelian variety over an algebraically closed field is constant --- is \emph{not} present in current Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) in any form usable here. The project will introduce it as a Lean-side declaration along the lines of
355	      \[
356	        \mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.AbelianVariety.constant\_of\_P1\_map}
357	      \]
358	      (final name and namespace to be fixed when the declaration is introduced into the Lean tree; the project intends this lemma as an upstream-Mathlib contribution candidate). When $g = \dim A = 0$ the statement is vacuous and the declaration's conclusion is automatic. The reduction step C.2.b reuses the project's already-formalised Theorem~\ref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} (with the noted ``group-object-on-source'' hypothesis handled either by a thin variant of the lemma or by inlining its proof template); the promotion step C.2.e uses standard reduced-source / separated-target lemmas, partially in Mathlib; the descent step C.2.f requires scheme-level Galois descent of morphism equality, also a Mathlib gap and itself a contribution candidate.
359	    \end{itemize}
360	
361	    \item[\textbf{(C.3)}] \emph{The trivial witness.} Take $J := \Spec k$, the terminal $k$-scheme. The group-object structure on $\Spec k$ is trivial; it is smooth proper geometrically irreducible of relative dimension $0 = g(C)$. The unique morphism $\iota_P \colon C \to \Spec k$ sends $P$ to the (unique) point. By Sub-step C.2 every $f \colon C \to A$ with $f(P) = \eta_A$ is constant at $\eta_A$, hence factors uniquely through the unique morphism $g \colon \Spec k \to A$ sending the point to $\eta_A$; uniqueness of $g$ follows because $\Spec k \to A$ is determined by its value on the unique $k$-rational point of $\Spec k$.
362	  \end{itemize}
363	
364	  \emph{Mathlib status for the genus-$0$ sub-case.}
365	  \begin{itemize}
366	    \item Sub-step C.1 requires the genus-$0$ identification on the curve side, which in turn requires Serre duality / the basic Riemann--Roch infrastructure that this project has explicitly trimmed (see the project's Phase~C exit policy). Mathlib has partial $\mathbb P^1$-theory but the curve-side genus-$0$ identification is missing.
367	    \item Sub-step C.2 reduces, via the project's already-formalised rigidity lemma (Theorem~\ref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} of Chapter~\ref{chap:Rigidity}), to the classical fact that proper rational curves on a positive-dimensional abelian variety over an algebraically closed field are constant (the keystone of Sub-step~C.2.d). This classical fact is not in Mathlib in the form needed here; see Sub-step~C.2.g for the explicit gap statement and contribution-candidate naming. Galois descent of morphism equality (Sub-step~C.2.f) is a second Mathlib gap, applied only in the residual sub-case $C(k) \neq \emptyset$ to transport the $\bar k$-side conclusion back to $k$.
368	    \item Sub-step C.3 is unconditional once C.1 and C.2 are in place.
369	  \end{itemize}
370	
371	  \paragraph{Mathlib infrastructure summary.}
372	  Three independent Mathlib build-outs each unlock one route of the proof:
373	  \begin{itemize}
374	    \item[\textbf{($\alpha$)}] \emph{Hilbert / Quot scheme infrastructure plus FGA representability}: unlocks Route A. The single highest-cost build-out, since Hilbert/Quot schemes are upstream of much of modern algebraic geometry; but once present, the rest of Route A (identity component, smoothness, properness, universal property) follows by classical arguments already widely formalised in adjacent settings.
375	    \item[\textbf{($\beta$)}] \emph{Symmetric powers of schemes plus finite-group-scheme quotients plus Stein factorisation for proper morphisms (with its cohomology-of-$\mathcal O_X$-modules hypotheses)}: unlocks Route B. Each piece is a meaningful Mathlib contribution in its own right; the bottleneck is the smooth-quotient property of $C^g / S_g$.
376	    \item[\textbf{($\gamma$)}] \emph{The rigidity theorem $\Hom(\mathbb P^1_k, A) = A(k)$ together with the genus-$0$ identification $C \cong \mathbb P^1_k$ for a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve with a $k$-rational point}: this is a prerequisite of the genus-$0$ sub-case under either Route A or Route B. Independently of ($\alpha$) and ($\beta$), establishing ($\gamma$) lets the genus-$0$ part of the witness be discharged unconditionally; the higher-genus part of the witness then remains under ($\alpha$) or ($\beta$).
377	  \end{itemize}
378	
379	  In sum: Mathlib currently contains none of the three infrastructure builds, and so the existence statement is recorded here as the project's single explicit foundational hypothesis. It backs all downstream consequences --- the four protected instances on $\Jac(C)$ (Theorems~\ref{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}--\ref{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred}), and the Abel--Jacobi map and its universal property of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} --- and is discharged by any one of the three routes above once the corresponding Mathlib infrastructure lands.
380	\end{proof}
381	
382	\section{Implementation route via the Albanese functor}
383	
384	Because the full Picard-scheme representability theorem is not available in current Mathlib, the formal construction proceeds in two layers:
385	
386	\begin{enumerate}
387	  \item \textbf{Layer I --- direct definition.} The Jacobian of $C$ is defined uniformly as the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness provided by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, carrying the four properties of Theorems~\ref{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}--\ref{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred} (each obtained by projection from the witness). The genus-$0$ case is included: the witness's universal property in that case implicitly carries the rigidity content $\Hom(\mathbb P^1_k, A) = A(k)$.
388	
389	  \item \textbf{Layer II --- Abel--Jacobi compatibility.} Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} introduces the Abel--Jacobi morphism $C \to \Jac(C)$ and shows that it is universal among morphisms from $C$ to abelian varieties.
390	\end{enumerate}
